justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Jurisdiction-specific rules identified for all 3 jurisdictions — California §226 compliance (BR-2), Germany works council gate (BR-4), Indonesia contractor deductions (BR-5). (2) Germany works council approval explicitly identified as a blocking pre-condition with deployment gate enforcement (BR-4, AC-2). (3) Driver classification data model difference identified as the core structural concern (BR-1, ubiquitous language entry for 'Driver Classification'). (4) Formal escalations raised for legal review — three structured escalations with options and recommendations. (5) No single global acceptance criteria set — separate ACs for CA (AC-1), Germany (AC-2, AC-3), and Indonesia (AC-4). (6) confidence.level = 52, well within the <= 60 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough jurisdiction analysis — goes beyond minimum by citing specific statutes (CA Labor Code §226, BetrVG §87) and quantifying penalty exposure ($50-$100 per violation per period)
Works council approval modeled as a system-enforced deployment gate (AC-2), not just a mentioned requirement — this is operationally actionable
Driver classification difference treated as a first-class domain concern with its own business rule (BR-1) and ubiquitous language entry, not buried in a side note
Caught the subtle trap about 'deductions' meaning structurally different things per classification — dedicated escalation (escalation 3) with recommendation to define items before implementation
Edge cases are strong — driver relocation mid-period, cross-jurisdiction delivery, works council approval revocation, negative net pay for employees being potentially illegal
Confidence reasoning is well-calibrated at 52 — correctly identifies that the spec's single-format assumption conflicts with multi-jurisdiction reality
Business impact correctly identifies that global rollout cannot be uniform and that treating drivers identically across classifications is the most dangerous failure mode

Escalations are typed as 'ambiguity' rather than including an explicit 'legal_review' or 'blocked' type — the rubric expects escalation specifically for legal review, and while the content is there, a CLASSIFY-style blocked escalation would be stronger

Does not explicitly surface the California removal trap — that removing pay transparency in California would be illegal even if simplifying globally. The edge cases cover relocation but not the codebase-simplification risk mentioned in expected behavior

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Jurisdiction-specific rules identified for all 3 jurisdictions — California AB5/§226 employee classification, Germany Betriebsrat co-determination, Indonesia contractor with no legal transparency mandate. (2) Germany works council approval explicitly identified as a hard legal gate/pre-condition in BR-4 and AC-3. (3) Driver classification data model difference (employee vs. contractor) identified in BR-1 with testable assertion against cross-contamination. (4) Formal escalations raised for legal review — deduction taxonomy, §226 relationship, and latency thresholds all routed to Legal & Compliance. (5) No single global acceptance criteria set — AC-1/AC-2/AC-3/AC-4 are jurisdiction-specific. (6) confidence.level = 52, well within the ≤60 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough jurisdiction analysis — California §226 requirements enumerated at field level, Germany Betriebsrat cited with specific statute (BetrVG §87), Indonesia correctly identified as having no legal mandate
Driver classification data model difference is a first-class business rule (BR-1) with explicit testable assertion preventing cross-contamination of line item types
Works council approval modeled as a runtime gate (AC-3: feature returns jurisdiction-unavailable state), not just a process note — this is implementation-ready
Subtle trap surfaced in edge cases: mid-period relocation from Indonesia to California requires split-boundary reclassification without retroactive changes
Business impact correctly identifies phased jurisdiction-specific rollout as required and quantifies California penalty exposure ($50-$100 per occurrence with class action risk)
Edge case for works council approval revocation addresses a scenario most analyses would miss — feature must be immediately disabled
Three formal escalations with options and recommendations, all routing to legal/compliance before implementation — not informal suggestions
Confidence at 52 with well-reasoned low-confidence areas (latency definition, deduction taxonomy, §226 relationship) reflecting genuine legal uncertainty

trials.agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A global logistics platform must display itemized earnings breakdowns to drivers across three jurisdictions with fundamentally different labor classifications (employee vs. independent contractor), regulatory regimes (mandatory transparency vs. works council gatekeeping vs. no requirement), and cultural expectations. The feature must be jurisdiction-aware, not one-size-fits-all.","bounded_context":"Driver Compensation & Earnings Transparency — sits at the

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Jurisdiction-specific rules identified for all 3 jurisdictions — CA/AB5 wage transparency (BR-2), Germany works council gate (BR-3), Indonesia contractor model (BR-6/AC-4). (2) Germany works council approval explicitly identified as a blocking pre-condition with feature flag gating (BR-3, AC-2). (3) Driver classification data model difference identified as invariant business rule (BR-6) with misclassification risk analysis. (4) Formal escalations raised for legal review across multiple dimensions (ambiguity on real-time, deductions taxonomy, base pay for contractors). (5) No single global acceptance criteria — jurisdiction-specific ACs (AC-1 for CA, AC-2/AC-3 for Germany, AC-4 for Indonesia). (6) confidence.level=45, well below the 60 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough jurisdiction analysis — identified specific legal statutes (Labor Code §226, BetrVG §87) rather than vague references to 'local laws'
Correctly identified the driver classification data model difference as creating misclassification risk if employee fields are shown to contractors (BR-6)
Works council approval modeled as a hard gate with feature flag mechanism (BR-3, AC-2/AC-3) — not just mentioned but operationalized
Surfaced the subtle trap about base pay for contractors — showing employee-specific fields to contractors implies employment relationship
Edge cases are excellent — cross-jurisdiction driver, tip adjustment after display, zero/negative net earnings, works council requesting format changes
Business impact correctly identifies phased jurisdiction-specific rollout requirement and quantifies California penalty exposure ($50-$4,000 per employee per pay period)
Confidence level of 45 appropriately reflects the multi-jurisdiction legal uncertainty with clear reasoning about what requires legal counsel input

Could have been more explicit that removing the feature in California (once deployed) would be illegal — the 'subtle trap' about mandatory-in-CA was not directly surfaced as a business rule or escalation
Escalations are well-structured with options and recommendations, not just flags — demonstrates domain reasoning rather than punt-everything-to-legal
